summary: Martin Fowler explains that hierarchic data models organize data as tree structures, noting they were common in early databases but fell out of favor as relational models dominated databases and network models dominated in-memory programming. The hierarchy's simplicity becomes a limitation with complex data. However, Fowler observes that XML's rise has brought hierarchic models back into relevance, offering standardized manipulation facilities comparable to SQL for relational data.
key_points: Hierarchic models organize data as trees but were displaced by relational (databases) and network (in-memory) models
Hierarchies break down when data complexity increases
XML has revived hierarchic data models by providing standard manipulation tools
The simplicity of hierarchies remains their enduring appeal despite limitations
money_quotes: A hierarchy, while a simple organizational tool, breaks down as you get more complex data.
The simplicity of a hierarchy is hard to deny, and hierarchic models keep showing value.
The big return of hierarchic data models these days is with the rise of XML.
key_insight: Despite being displaced by more flexible data models, hierarchic structures persist because their simplicity provides real value, as demonstrated by XML's success.
